hook: You already know how to work with graphs — vertices, edges, paths, and connectivity. You can tell whether a graph is connected and whether it contains cycles. But here's a puzzle: a telecommunications company wants to connect 10 cities with cables so that every city can reach every other city, yet they want to use as few cables as possible to minimize cost. How many cables do they need, and what structure must the network have? You don't yet have the tools to answer this precisely.
concept: A tree is a connected graph with no cycles — the simplest possible structure that keeps everything reachable without any redundancy. Formally, a tree is a connected, acyclic undirected graph $T = (V, E)$. A spanning tree of a graph $G$ is a subgraph that is a tree and includes every vertex of $G$. Every connected graph has at least one spanning tree.
anatomy: A tree on $n$ vertices has exactly $n - 1$ edges — this edge count is a defining property, not a coincidence. The vertices of degree 1 are called leaves (or leaf nodes), and they are the 'endpoints' of the tree. Internal vertices are all non-leaf vertices. A root is a distinguished vertex chosen to impose a parent-child hierarchy, turning an unrooted tree into a rooted tree. In a spanning tree of $G = (V, E)$, the tree edges are the $|V| - 1$ edges selected, while the remaining edges of $G$ are called non-tree edges or back edges.

worked_example.expression_latex: G = (V, E), \quad V = \{A, B, C, D, E\}, \quad E = \{AB, AC, BC, CD, DE, BD\}
worked_example.description_latex: We start with the graph $G$ having 5 vertices and 6 edges. Before finding a spanning tree, notice that $|V| = 5$, so any spanning tree must use exactly $|V| - 1 = 4$ edges. This gives us a target to aim for.
We initialize the spanning tree with all 5 vertices but no edges yet. We will add edges one at a time, being careful never to create a cycle, until we have exactly 4 edges and the graph is connected.

We add edge $AB$ (connects $A$ and $B$, no cycle), then $AC$ (connects $A$ and $C$, no cycle), then $CD$ (connects $C$ and $D$, no cycle), then $DE$ (connects $D$ and $E$, no cycle). At each step we check: would adding this edge create a cycle? Since all four edges connect previously disconnected components, no cycle forms.

Our spanning tree $T$ now has 5 vertices and 4 edges, which matches the required $n - 1 = 4$. Every vertex is reachable from every other: for example, $B \to A \to C \to D \to E$. We did not include edges $BC$ or $BD$ because adding either would create a cycle.

The final verification checks all three properties of a spanning tree: it uses every vertex of $G$, it is connected, and it contains no cycles. Confirming $|E_T| = n - 1$ is a quick sanity check — if this count is wrong, something went awry.

partial_example.description_latex: We are given a graph $G$ with 4 vertices and 4 edges. Our goal is to find a spanning tree. First, determine how many edges the spanning tree must contain: a spanning tree on $n$ vertices always has exactly $n - 1$ edges.

We correctly identify that the spanning tree needs exactly 3 edges. Now we must choose 3 of the 4 available edges such that the result is connected and acyclic. Identify which edge, if included, would create a cycle alongside two other edges, and decide whether to exclude it.
Write down one valid spanning tree $T$ by listing its 3 edges, and explain in one sentence why the edge you omitted had to be left out (i.e., which cycle would it have formed?).

practice_problems.prompt_latex: A tree has 8 vertices. How many edges does it have?
Determine whether the graph $G$ with $V = \{A,B,C,D\}$ and $E = \{AB, BC, CD\}$ is a tree. Justify your answer.
practice_problems.answer_latex: Yes, $G$ is a tree: it has 4 vertices and 3 = 4 - 1 edges, it is connected (every vertex reachable from every other), and it is acyclic.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: Find ALL distinct spanning trees of the complete graph $K_3$ (triangle: vertices $\{1,2,3\}$, edges $\{12, 13, 23\}$).
practice_problems.answer_latex: There are 3 spanning trees, each omitting one edge: $\{12,13\}$, $\{12,23\}$, and $\{13,23\}$.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: A connected graph $G$ has $n$ vertices and $m$ edges. A spanning tree $T$ of $G$ is found. How many non-tree edges (edges in $G$ but not in $T$) are there? Express your answer in terms of $m$ and $n$.

A network has 6 routers, and the IT team wants to connect them all using the minimum number of direct links so that data can travel between any two routers (possibly through others). If they later discover one link is faulty and remove it, is the network still guaranteed to be connected? Explain.
practice_problems.answer_latex: The minimum number of links is $6 - 1 = 5$, forming a spanning tree. No — removing any single edge from a tree disconnects it, since trees have no redundant paths. The network would not be guaranteed to stay connected.

common_mistakes: Students often think any subgraph with $n-1$ edges is a spanning tree. This is wrong: a graph can have $n-1$ edges and still be disconnected (e.g., two separate components). You need $n-1$ edges AND connectivity — or equivalently, $n-1$ edges AND acyclicity, which together force connectivity.

When building a spanning tree by adding edges, students sometimes forget to check for cycles and accidentally include a redundant edge, ending up with $n-1$ edges but a cycle inside. Always verify: if adding an edge would connect two vertices already in the same component, it creates a cycle and must be skipped.

Students confuse 'a spanning tree of $G$' with 'the spanning tree of $G$.' Most connected graphs have many distinct spanning trees (Cayley's formula gives $n^{n-2}$ for $K_n$). Saying 'find the spanning tree' as if there is only one is imprecise — there are typically many valid answers.
untested_variants: Applying Kruskal's or Prim's algorithm to find a minimum spanning tree (MST) of a weighted graph — the practice problems here involve unweighted graphs only.
Using Cayley's formula $n^{n-2}$ to count the exact number of labeled spanning trees of the complete graph $K_n$ for $n \geq 4$.
Proving the equivalence of the three characterizations of trees: (1) connected and acyclic, (2) connected with $n-1$ edges, (3) acyclic with $n-1$ edges.
Rooted trees and tree traversal algorithms (DFS/BFS spanning trees), where the direction of traversal determines which edges become tree edges versus back edges.
